claim: A man who gave dāna, kept sīla and uposatha aspiring to the nāga-world was reborn in the nāgabhavana as Saṅkhapāla nāgarāja.
note: explicit karma->nāga rebirth path

claim: Hunters plan to kill and eat the nāga-king as game meat.

claim: The nāgabhavana is described as like Mt Sineru, a celestial Tāvatiṃsa-like palace-realm under the water.

claim: The nāgabhavana has lapis-lazuli lotus pools, mango groves, sorrow-vanishing on entry.

claim: The nāgabhavana holds jewelled gabled mansions, silver doors, a thousand-pillared palace.

claim: As the nāga Saṅkhapāla the bodhisatta fulfilled all ten perfections (giving his body etc.).

claim: Purity/path-fruit-Nibbāna and the arising of Buddhas and paccekabuddhas happen only in the human world, not the nāga-realm.

claim: A widowed nāga-maiden leaves the nāgabhavana, roams the seashore in human-like form, and follows a prince's footprints.
note: Bhūridatta-jātaka opening; nāga-maiden in human form on land

claim: The nāga-maiden conceives by the human prince and bears a son and daughter (human-natured children).
note: nāga-human union producing offspring; cf p006

claim: The nāga-children are human-natured by their father's seed but are water-born and could die of sun/wind on the road.

claim: Despite great power, the nāga-king is unworthy of the human princess because of his snake-birth (ahijātikattā).
note: explicit lower birth-class status of nāga
nāga-youths refrain from killing, vanish; narrative
nāgas ask whether to harm anyone; narrative dialogue

claim: Nāga-kings receive the princess; nāga-maidens disguise themselves as human attendants; she feels divine touch on the celestial bed.

claim: The nāga-king (Bhūridatta) undertakes the four-factored uposatha on an anthill, offering his skin/sinew/bone/blood, keeping the observance for a long time.
note: explicit nāga uposatha observance; Uposathakaṇḍa

claim: The bodhisatta-nāga makes his snake body vanish and manifests an adorned divine (human-form) body; nāga-maidens worship him.
note: explicit shapeshift snake->divine form; Garuḷakhaṇḍa

claim: The bodhisatta declares his true nāga-king status to the brahmin.
note: nāgarājabhāvaṃ kathento; self-declaration of being a nāga

claim: The nāga claims that by his fiery power (tejas) he could reduce a whole country to ashes; he is known throughout the 500-league nāgabhavana.
note: nāga venom/fire power + realm extent

gloss on dwelling happily in nāgabhavana; narrative gloss

claim: Brought to the nāgabhavana, the brahmin and son receive divine bodies; the bodhisatta-nāga gives divine wealth and nāga-maidens, keeps the uposatha heedfully.
note: realm grants dibba attabhāva; also nāga uposatha

claim: The nāga's mansion has lapis pillars, thousand-pillared palaces full of nāga-maidens, like Sakka's Vejayanta; his iddhi is vast.

claim: The pure-natured nāga-king, fearing breach of his precept, does not get angry or even open his eyes though force-fed venom-drug; the charmer abuses his body with impunity.
note: nāga venom-power restrained by sīla; sīlabheda-fear

claim: The captured nāga-king is fed honey-grain and killed frogs, refuses food, and is made to perform in villages.

claim: Unlike his brothers who stay in the nāgabhavana, this nāga goes to the human world to keep the uposatha because of his virtuous disposition.

claim: One of the four, having made merit and aspired to that station, is reborn at life's end as a nāga-king with wife and sons in the nāgabhavana.
note: explicit karma+aspiration -> nāga-king rebirth

claim: Varuṇa the nāga-king, having undertaken the uposatha, leaves the nāgabhavana (a hindrance there) for a pleasant spot in the human world to do the recluse-practice.
note: nāga uposatha + realm as palibodha

claim: The nāga-king's distinctive virtue is khanti (forbearance), set beside the garuḷa, gandhabba and human kings.
note: defining trait attributed to nāgas as a class; cf p1213/p1233

claim: The nāga-king praises adhivāsana-khanti (non-anger toward provoking persons) as his characteristic virtue.
note: class trait gloss
